result.chapter_summary: Chapter 6 argues that while the Common Core State Standards contain 'golden words' about the necessity of a content-rich curriculum, they are being undermined by a continued operational focus on content-free skills. The author contends that because the standards do not mandate specific, grade-by-grade knowledge, test-makers default to measuring pseudo-skills like 'close reading' and 'finding the main idea,' perpetuating the same cycle of educational failure seen in previous standards.

claims.text: The Common Core State Standards in language arts can improve American education significantly only if interpreted as a mandate for building knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The Common Core State Standards in language arts can improve American education significantly, but only if we interpret them productively.

claims.text: Common Core standards are similar to failed earlier state standards because they do not state the specific knowledge students should gain.
claims.source_passage: They are superior to earlier state standards in some respects but all too similar to them in not stating the specific knowledge that students should gain.

claims.text: Improvement in students' verbal abilities depends on their knowledge and vocabulary size rather than practicing reading skills.
claims.source_passage: Yet real improvement in students’ verbal abilities will not depend on practicing these skills; it will depend on how much students know and how big their vocabularies are.

claims.text: The long-range success of the Common Core depends on requiring a coherent approach to building knowledge in elementary school.
claims.source_passage: The long-range educational success of the new Common Core Standards in language arts will depend almost completely on interpreting them as requiring a coherent approach to building knowledge in the elementary school.

claims.text: Continuing to emphasize skills over knowledge will cause the Common Core to fail in improving student verbal abilities.
claims.source_passage: If, as is currently happening, the schools continue to emphasize skills, the new standards will fail to enlarge students’ vocabularies significantly and will therefore fail to improve verbal abilities significantly.

claims.text: Content-indeterminate standards and tests cause schools to de-emphasize systematic knowledge gain.
claims.source_passage: Content-indeterminate standards plus content-indeterminate tests have caused schools to de-emphasize systematic knowledge gain.

claims.text: Standards must be complemented by a well-developed, content-rich curriculum to be effective.
claims.source_passage: The Standards . . . do not—indeed, cannot—enumerate all or even most of the content that students should learn. The Standards must therefore be complemented by a well-developed, content-rich curriculum.

claims.text: Students gain a foundation of knowledge only when the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured across grades.
claims.source_passage: Students can only gain this foundation when the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured to develop rich content knowledge within and across grades.

claims.text: The knowledge-centric statements in the Common Core have no direct operational impact on tests.
claims.source_passage: They have no direct operational impact on tests. Although the statements fairly shout that “rich” knowledge and vocabulary are the goals toward which schools should aim, those sentiments cannot affect the topics of test items

claims.text: Test makers cannot include specific topics on tests because they cannot know which topics individual schools have taught.
claims.source_passage: since test makers cannot know which topics in each grade level are the ones to which “an adequate number of titles” have been devoted in the schools.

claims.text: The Common Core standard requiring 'logical inferences' is defective because textual inferences are contextual rather than logical.
claims.source_passage: This logical inference standard is defective on two grounds. . . the inferences one draws in interpretation are not logical, except in a few specialized texts involving syllogisms or other formal principles.

claims.text: A text does not say anything 'explicitly' until it has been interpreted by the reader.
claims.source_passage: No text says anything “explicitly” until after it has been interpreted.

claims.text: The 'close-reading' skill distracts conscious attention away from the message of a text to its medium of expression.
claims.source_passage: But paying conscious attention to surface features can be a quite cumbersome habit since it distracts conscious attention away from the message to its medium of expression

claims.text: Raising Lexile levels of random, disconnected texts will not automatically increase student knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: There’s little reason to hope that this will happen simply from raising the Lexile levels of helter-skelter texts.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a general skill, whereas listening comprehension is recognized as topic-dependent.
claims.source_passage: The policy aim of test uniformity was predicated on the widely held but mistaken idea that reading comprehension is a general skill. Would the mistake have been made if the skill were listening comprehension? Probably not, since common sense would urge that listening comprehension is topic-dependent.

claims.text: The Common Core standards have added a 'close-reading' skill that requires students to make logical inferences from text.
claims.source_passage: To this main-idea-finding skill, the new Core standards have added a ‘close-�reading’ skill requiring students to make ‘logical’ inferences from the text.

claims.text: Operationally, text 'complexity' in the Common Core has been defined as using texts with higher Lexile scores.
claims.source_passage: Operationally this has meant using texts that have higher Lexile scores than schools used before, a potential step forward to the extent that it builds student knowledge and vocabulary more rapidly.

claims.text: Common Core standards have made tests harder to pass without improving the actual ability of students to pass them.
claims.source_passage: The net result of the new standards so far has been to make the tests harder to pass, but not to make students better able to pass them.

claims.text: Teaching formal close-reading skills as a preparation for tests may lower mature reading scores by consuming class time.
claims.source_passage: I will explain in this chapter why the addition of close reading to main-idea finding will turn out in the end not to raise mature reading scores, and could even lower them by encouraging schools to usurp yet more class time in practicing formal close-reading skills in preparation for close-reading test items.

claims.text: Common Core developers omitted specific content to ensure political adoption by over forty states.
claims.source_passage: They knew that including definite text titles would exclude gaining the adherence of over forty states, a remarkable achievement.

claims.text: Reading proficiency does not require mastering a specific literary canon.
claims.source_passage: It is not necessary for a student to master a specific literary canon to score well on a reading test, nor to be college and career ready, nor to become a competent citizen.

claims.text: Reading proficiency requires mastering a specific canon of knowledge and vocabulary shared by competent readers.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, there is a canon of knowledge and vocabulary known to all highly competent readers and writers. That is axiomatic; possessing that knowledge is what makes them competent.

claims.text: Reading tests are actually knowledge tests in disguise.
claims.source_passage: ‘Reading tests are knowledge tests in disguise’ is Daniel Willingham’s elegant formulation.

claims.text: Reading tests must use passages on domains not studied in school because the school curriculum is unstated and unknown to test makers.
claims.source_passage: They are compelled to offer texts on domains of knowledge not studied in school, since the school domains are unstated and unknown.

claims.text: Only curriculum-based reading tests can be fair and educationally productive.
claims.source_passage: If the test is not curriculum based, it will not be productive.

claims.text: States and localities are currently the only jurisdictions capable of creating fair and productive tests because only they determine curriculum content.
claims.source_passage: Since only states and localities can currently determine the knowledge domains of the curriculum, they are currently the only jurisdictions in a position to make fair and productive tests.

claims.text: No text has 'explicit' meaning until it has been interpreted through a situation model.
claims.source_passage: No text says anything ‘explicitly’ until after it has been interpreted.

claims.text: Nationwide reading tests that are both fair and productive of sound school practice are a political impossibility as long as a nationwide elementary content core is absent.
claims.source_passage: as long as a nationwide elementary content core is a political impossibility, we will have to give up the pipe dream of nationwide reading tests that are both fair and productive of sound school practice.

claims.text: The aim of creating better readers trumps the policy aim of establishing a common yardstick for state-to-state measurement.
claims.source_passage: But getting a common yardstick and more accurate data is not as important an aim as making better readers.

claims.text: Valid high-stakes reading tests must be composed of passages that align with the specific domains of the local curriculum.
claims.source_passage: To be educationally valid, high-stakes reading tests would need to be composed of passages on domains from the local curriculum.

claims.text: The poor performance of seventeen-year-olds on the NAEP and SAT suggests that general reading skills do not exist as taught.
claims.source_passage: If such all-purpose reading skills existed, our seventeen-year-olds, who have practiced such skills for years, would be performing better on NAEP and the SAT, not worse.

claims.text: The all-purpose skills advocated in the Common Core language arts standards are myths.
claims.source_passage: the all-purpose skills advocated in the Common Core language arts standards are largely myths just like the all-purpose skills of earlier state standards.

claims.text: There is no general main-idea-finding skill, complexity-managing skill, or close-reading skill.
claims.source_passage: I will show that there is no general main-idea-finding skill, no complexity-managing skill, and no close-reading skill.

claims.text: The knowledge-based character of verbal skills is an unalterable principle that policy makers cannot repeal.
claims.source_passage: It is not in the power of any policy maker, legislator, standards maker, or anyone else involved in educational policy to repeal the knowledge-based character of verbal skills.

claims.text: The main-idea-finding skill was devised for the convenience of test makers rather than to benefit students.
claims.source_passage: the chief virtue of the main-idea concept is its convenience for grownups, not for students, who have not been much helped under its regime.

claims.text: The ability to find the main idea of a text depends entirely on the student's prior knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: But there is no significant main-idea-finding skill apart from the prior ability to comprehend a text—an ability that depends on the relevant knowledge and vocabulary already possessed by the student taking the test.

claims.text: Main-idea finding is a circular skill: you cannot find the main idea until you already understand what the text means.
claims.source_passage: From an analytical view, the main-idea-finding skill is a circularity. You can’t deduce the main idea until you have already construed what the text means.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children would perform better on tests if they studied subject matter rather than practicing finding main ideas.
claims.source_passage: They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school. And in that case they would not need to practice finding the main idea.

claims.text: Close reading is often implemented in ways that distract students from the actual message of the text.
claims.source_passage: paying conscious attention to surface features can be a quite cumbersome habit since it distracts conscious attention away from the message to its medium of expression

claims.text: Students lacking requisite knowledge and vocabulary will not be able to answer reading comprehension questions regardless of skill practice.
claims.source_passage: acking req- uisite knowledge and vocabulary will not be able to answer the ques- tion. They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school.

claims.text: Studying question-relevant subject matter is more effective for reading performance than practicing finding the main idea.
claims.source_passage: They would perform far better on the passage if they had studied question-relevant subject matter in school. And in that case they would not need to practice finding the main idea.

claims.text: Close reading is a specialized activity suitable for advanced literary analysis in later grades, not for early reading instruction.
claims.source_passage: Close reading is an activity that is suitable for appreciating the finer points of literature in later grades... But to encourage the practice as a mode of actual reading of texts or the testing of reading comprehension is a mistake unjustified by credible research.

claims.text: Close reading produces inferior comprehension and memory for meaning compared to reading for general meaning.
claims.source_passage: Close reading had produced inferior comprehension and memory for meaning.

claims.text: Paying close attention to surface text debases comprehension by consuming limited working memory resources.
claims.source_passage: With most texts, paying close attention to the text itself debases comprehension by usurping limited mental resources that would be bet- ter applied to pondering the substantive implications and the validity of what the text is saying.

claims.text: Human working memory is limited.
claims.source_passage: The first is that hu- man working memory is limited.

claims.text: People remember what they pay attention to; focusing on words prevents readers from considering important unstated implications.
claims.source_passage: The second finding is that we remember what we pay attention to. If we pay attention to words, those are what we will consider and remember rather than important unstated implications.

claims.text: Interpreted meaning is not reliably bound to the specific words that produce it.
claims.source_passage: Recent work on text comprehension shows that interpreted meaning is not reliably bound to the words that produce it

claims.text: Ambiguity is a critical feature of human language that allows for flexibility.
claims.source_passage: That’s because words are ambiguous—an absolutely critical feature of human language that allows it to become a hugely flexible instrument.

claims.text: The relationship between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.
claims.source_passage: since the relation between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.

claims.text: Inferences drawn from text are contextual and dependent on situation models rather than being strictly logical deductions from words.
claims.source_passage: The inferences we usually draw from a text are not logical but con- textual—dependent upon the situation model that we construct, not upon the mere words on the page.

claims.text: A text does not speak its own meaning; meaning is constructed by combining text with the reader's unspoken knowledge.
claims.source_passage: the text does not speak its own meaning. That meaning is a construct composed of what the text says plus the addi- tional relevant, unspoken knowledge that the reader brings to it.

claims.text: Close reading is often used by readers merely to find support for meanings they have already acquired.
claims.source_passage: When I examine what people actually do when they use close reading in order to write about texts, I find that they use the close-reading technique to support the meanings that they have already gained before they began to close-read.

claims.text: The close-reading strand in the Common Core is scientifically questionable.
claims.source_passage: The unfortunate consequences of the scientifically questionable close-reading strand in the Common Core is the effect it has had on the tests and the time-wasting preparation for them.

claims.text: The PARCC assessments employ defective test items that assume meaning can be logically inferred from textual 'clues' in isolation.
claims.source_passage: The Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers (PARCC), one of the two Common Core test consortia, has created two-part close- reading questions. One part asks about the meaning of a word, the other about the feature of the text from which one can logically infer that meaning.

claims.text: Standardized test questions that ask students to infer word meanings solely from context are based on an inadequate concept of reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The public has already started to protest these odd and sometimes unanswerable test items that have been constructed on an inadequate concept of reading comprehension.

claims.text: Logical inference derived purely from close reading is a 'mirage' because understanding depends on prior knowledge of word meanings.
claims.source_passage: No, her answer that misery means exhaustion is wrong because logical inference from close reading is a mirage. What she needed to know to answer the question was the usual meaning of the word misery.

claims.text: Paying attention to specific word features is a less effective strategy than reading for meaning.
claims.source_passage: This close-reading standard in the Common Core should be recast (or excised) to conform with the scientific finding that paying attention to word features is less effective than reading for meaning

claims.text: Common Core's focus on complex texts is a technical attempt to improve comprehension while avoiding the political difficulty of prescribing specific knowledge domains.
claims.source_passage: It tries to induce a more challenging and productive curriculum under the political constraint that the actual knowledge domains and texts of the curriculum must not be prescribed.

claims.text: A primary cause for the decline in reading abilities was the use of overly undemanding texts in schools.
claims.source_passage: One of the theories for the decline in reading abilities had been that the texts students have been asked to read are too undemanding.

claims.text: Lexile measures were historically used to 'dumb down' the elementary curriculum.
claims.source_passage: It would follow, then, that if in the past we used readability measures like Lexiles to dumb down the elementary curriculum, we might use those same measures at a higher level to smarten up the curriculum.

claims.text: Students are often capable of handling more difficult texts than educators typically assume.
claims.source_passage: But in actuality they can do more than we often think they can.

claims.text: Student progress with complex texts is strictly limited when the topic of the text is unfamiliar, regardless of its technical difficulty score.
claims.source_passage: But there are strict limits to the progress students can make if the text, regardless of its Lexile score, is on a topic that is unfamiliar.

claims.text: The skills-based approach in the Common Core conflicts with its requirement for a rich, coherent knowledge curriculum.
claims.source_passage: This is where the knowledge imperative and the skill imperative of the Common Core come into conflict. Intellectually, the skills-approach lives uncomfortably with the new, more communal approach of Common Core Standards that require a rich, coherent curriculum.

claims.text: Lexiles and complexity measures are indifferent to subject matter and can be used to avoid content-rich curricula.
claims.source_passage: Complexity and Lexiles are largely indifferent to subject matter. They can equally sponsor a coherent curriculum or a fragmented one. That is why they can be used as a technical crutch for content avoidance

claims.text: The individualism of classroom libraries is antithetical to the rich communal curriculum required by the Common Core standards.
claims.source_passage: Such individualism is antithetical to the rich communal curriculum required by the standards.

claims.text: The only way to effectively implement a rich, coherent curriculum is for students in early grades to read mostly the same texts.
claims.source_passage: The requirement that 'the curriculum is intentionally and coherently structured to develop rich content knowledge within and across grades' can only be fulfilled if students in the early grades mostly read the same texts.

claims.text: A student's ability to master complex syntax and rare vocabulary increases as they become familiar with the subject matter.
claims.source_passage: As topics become familiar, student ability to master rarer words and longer sentences (the chief elements of complexity) increases.

claims.text: The Common Core standards contain an internal self-contradiction regarding the structure of the curriculum and student text selection.
claims.source_passage: This self-contradiction within the Common Core Standards should be resolved in favor of commonality of texts in the classroom, since that’s the only way to effectively implement a rich, coherent, and cumulative curriculum.

claims.text: Domain immersion—using a sequence of texts from the same domain—induces progress in reading complex texts.
claims.source_passage: It induces progress in reading complex texts not because an all-purpose complexity-managing skill has increased in two weeks, but because students’ domain familiarity has increased.

claims.text: There is no such thing as an all-purpose, content-independent complexity-managing skill.
claims.source_passage: Getting familiar with a lot of domains is the best way to enhance ability to read complex texts, since there is no all-purpose complexity-managing skill.

claims.text: Domain familiarity is the primary skill required to manage text complexity.
claims.source_passage: Domain familiarity is the chief complexity-managing skill.

claims.text: Schools should prioritize the acquisition of knowledge as the primary method for improving reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: the reliable way to achieve technical reading comprehension skills for all students, including the ability to read highly complex and challenging texts, is for the school to stress knowledge front and center as the chief reading comprehension skill

claims.text: The Common Core requirement that roughly half of texts be informational is one of its most beneficial standards.
claims.source_passage: The most criticized element in the Common Core language arts standards is one of the best standards of all—that about half of the texts read should be “informational” texts.

claims.text: Increasing informational texts reduces the prevalence of vacuous stories and enhances systematic knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: This immediately reduces the number of vacuous stories about picnics at the beach with the Beaver family, and it potentially enhances the curriculum with more systematic knowledge and vocabulary in multiple domains.

claims.text: The distinction between 'literary' and 'informational' texts is artificial; good fiction provides information and good information can be literature.
claims.source_passage: The distinction need not be between “literary” and “informational” texts. Good works of fiction are informational. And good informational texts can be literature.

claims.text: Narratives are the most effective psychological format for conveying knowledge to students.
claims.source_passage: Narratives, fictional or nonfictional, are psychologically “privileged.” They are the most effective way to convey knowledge.

claims.text: The quality of fictional literature depends on its aim toward truth and its grounding in factual knowledge of the world.
claims.source_passage: if fiction isn’t informational, it usually isn’t very good. Good fictional stories and novels aim at truth.

claims.text: Understanding literature like 'To Kill a Mockingbird' requires significant prior knowledge of history and social context that should be taught in earlier grades.
claims.source_passage: To Kill a Mockingbird, widely taught in seventh grade, depends on students’ having a lot of prior knowledge about the social and historical world that should have been gained in the earlier grades.

claims.text: Deep comprehension of literary texts requires specific background knowledge in history, geography, sociology, science, and technology.
claims.source_passage: Fictional stories are composed in historical time. They allude to history, geography, sociology, science, and technology.

claims.text: Fiction and poetry are not fact-free uses of language that can be mastered through content-independent word study and comprehension sessions.
claims.source_passage: Fiction and poetry are not fact-free uses of language that can be readily understood after the requisite number of word study and comprehension sessions.

claims.text: Before the Romantic movement, the term 'literature' was used broadly to include almost all genres of writing.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, before the romantic movement the word literature embraced almost all genres of writing.

claims.text: Good literature aims to communicate truth in a manner comparable to history and science.
claims.source_passage: Good fiction and poetry aim at truth no less than do history and science.

claims.text: The visualization of 'imaginative' worlds in literature still depends on the reader's knowledge of the real world.
claims.source_passage: even fairy tales and science fiction, depend on knowledge of this world in order to be realized in the imagination of the reader.

claims.text: Early American literacy materials, such as Noah Webster's and W.H. McGuffey's readers, integrated poetry and fiction with biographies, history, and science to form moral and patriotic citizens.
claims.source_passage: In earlier times, the readings in Noah Webster’s Blue Back Speller and W. H. McGuffey’s Eclectic Readers were directed to making moral and patriotic citizens. They included poetry and fiction, but also biographies of great persons, histories of the United States, science, health, and moral tales.

claims.text: The educational goal of 'cultivating the imagination' became an accepted standard in the United States around the 1850s due to European Romantic influence.
claims.source_passage: The fairy tale proponents won out in due course, because by the 1850s “cultivation of the imagination” had started to become an accepted educational goal.

claims.text: American early literacy instruction has been dominated by a near-monopoly of 'imaginative literature' for over a century.
claims.source_passage: For more than a century in the United States a dominant emphasis in early literacy classes has been placed on “imaginative literature,” with a high value given to developing the child’s “creativity and imagination.”

claims.text: The fierce opposition to Common Core’s inclusion of informational text is caused by the historical shift toward Romantic educational ideals.
claims.source_passage: It explains the almost hysterical response to the Common Core’s breaking up the near-monopoly of fiction and poetry in language arts.

claims.text: The view that balanced literacy (50/50 fiction and nonfiction) is sacrilegious is a result of Romantic pantheism rather than traditional educational values.
claims.source_passage: The view that a 50/50 balance between fiction and nonfiction should seem sacrilegious to language arts teachers is entirely owing to the influence of European romanticism on American education.

claims.text: Biblical and early American educational perspectives viewed 'imagination' as potentially deceptive or evil rather than inherently virtuous.
claims.source_passage: The Bible says that “the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth.” No positive uses of the word imagine are to be found in the Bible.

claims.text: The contemporary assumption that imagination is always positive and life-enhancing is an uncritical belief derived from Romanticism.
claims.source_passage: But the idea that imagination is always positive and life-enhancing is an uncritical assumption that has crept into our discourse from the pantheistic effusions of the romantic period

claims.text: The educational focus on developing imagination represents a shift from communal education to the Romantic idea of natural individualism.
claims.source_passage: The educational aim of developing the child’s imagination represented a shift from the communal conception of early education to the romantic idea of natural individua

claims.text: Teaching children substantial knowledge about the world is more valuable to their well-being, creativity, and imagination than emphasizing fanciful stories intended to encourage imagination.
claims.source_passage: teaching them substantial knowledge about the world is far more valuable to their future well-being—and to their creativity and imagination—than emphasizing fanciful and sometimes not-very-good stories whose only virtue is that they are 'imaginative'

claims.text: Coherent integration of nonfiction, poetry, and fiction in the elementary curriculum makes children better communicators and more active citizens.
claims.source_passage: if it is applied in a coherent way and integrated with the other disciplines in the elementary curriculum, children will become much abler communicators. And that... will make them better students and wealthier, more active citizens—and abler readers of literature.

claims.text: The American public's initial approval of the Common Core was based on the belief that it meant a common grade-by-grade curriculum.
claims.source_passage: They thought it meant a core of common subject matter grade-by-grade in the elementary school, and that idea made sense to them. They did not know that the language arts standards were empty of specific grade-by-grade content.

claims.text: The concept of commonality in early schooling is a deeply rooted American tradition.
claims.source_passage: The idea of commonality in early schooling is as American as cherry pie.

claims.text: During the 'Silver Age' of American education (1930s-40s), textbooks provided a national curriculum that balanced localism with federalism.
claims.source_passage: Federalism was acknowledged through the use of deliberately communal textbooks like the Everyday Classics Series and other, similar books that had wide currency throughout the schools of the nation.

claims.text: The public elementary school is the primary remaining institution capable of ensuring all children learn the shared knowledge required for a nation to be operational.
claims.source_passage: It is still the place where all children have the possibility of learning the shared knowledge and values that make a nation operational.

claims.text: American competence depends upon mastering the national dimension of the public sphere rather than local or regional variations.
claims.source_passage: What makes an American competent—whether in Kittery, Maine, or Cleveland, Ohio, or Oakland, California—is mastery of the national dimension of our public sphere.

claims.text: The modern nation-state requires a standardized national language created through school-based instruction.
claims.source_passage: When the modern nation state was created in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Europe, each nation instituted a standardized national language to be promulgated and learned in the schools.

claims.text: American English is a deliberate, invented national culture founded on the work of Dr. Johnson and Noah Webster.
claims.source_passage: Today we learn and speak, read and write Johnsonian-Websterese. I use this invented term to stress the deliberate, invented character of a shared national culture

claims.text: The creation of the modern nation-state in the 17th and 18th centuries necessitated the institution of standardized national languages in schools.
claims.source_passage: When the modern nation state was created in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries in Europe, each nation instituted a standardized national language to be promulgated and learned in the schools.

claims.text: Modern nations depend on authoritative dictionaries to stabilize pronunciation, grammar, and vocabulary to enable communication across space and time.
claims.source_passage: the formation of the modern nation depended upon authoritative dictionary makers to stabilize pronunciation, grammar, spelling, and word meaning so people could reliably communicate over space and time.

claims.text: Standard American English refers to a normalization of unspoken knowledge as much as it refers to grammar and spelling.
claims.source_passage: Since the 1960s a further insight has become widely accepted among socio- and psycholinguists—that the normalization of language has entailed a normalization of the unspoken knowledge that makes the language meaningful to its users.

claims.text: The formal register of language taught in schools is the essential instrument of communication in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: This formal register (taught in schools) is the instrument of communication in the public sphere.

claims.text: A student cannot master Standard American English without mastering standard American knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In short, a student cannot master Standard American English without mastering standard American knowledge.

claims.text: Global education must be conducted through the medium of a national language built upon shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: But a global education must be conducted through the medium of a national language built upon shared knowledge.

claims.text: Unspoken knowledge is a necessary, silent component of every language transaction.
claims.source_passage: Unspoken knowledge must be silently shared in every language transaction.

claims.text: Schools that ignore the necessity of shared knowledge exclude disadvantaged children from language mastery.
claims.source_passage: When schools ignore the educational imperatives of that psychological truth, they exclude disadvantaged children from language mastery.

claims.text: Providing universal linguistic competence via shared knowledge is the foundational duty of democratic elementary schools.
claims.source_passage: To provide this universal linguistic competence is the foundational aim and duty of the elementary schools of a democracy.

claims.text: The concept of 'spontaneity' in education falsely assumes the child's brain develops like a ball of string unwinding naturally.
claims.source_passage: It produced curious aberrations like ‘spontaneity,’ which supposes that the child’s brain is like a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.

claims.text: Education is a struggle against elemental biological instincts intended to produce an 'up-to-date' person for the current era.
claims.source_passage: Education must oppose the elemental biological instincts of nature; it is a struggle against nature, to dominate it and produce the ‘up-to-date’ person of the new era.

claims.text: The educational concept of child development as a 'natural unfolding' has interfered with the primary goal of early education, which is socialization.
claims.source_passage: Gramsci makes a key observation about why the new ideas about child development as an unfolding of the child have interfered with the chief point of early education—socialization

claims.text: Schools must constantly update the shared, taken-for-granted knowledge of the 'new era' to ensure community and competence.
claims.source_passage: The cloud of taken-for-granted knowledge in “the new era” constantly needs updating in the schools to ensure competence and community.

claims.text: Imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere should not be considered cultural imperialism.
claims.source_passage: We must not be intimidated by the reproach that imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere is “cultural imperialism.”

claims.text: The United States was historically conceived as an 'internal empire' of quasi-sovereign states rather than an external imperial power.
claims.source_passage: The United States was conceived as a mutually beneficial internal empire of quasi-sovereign states. Some of the founders used the term empire for the nation as an internal, not external, idea.

claims.text: Universities should create an inventory of the specific knowledge and vocabulary required for college and career readiness.
claims.source_passage: The universities could greatly help education and the public sphere by creating an inventory of the knowledge and vocabulary that makes a person college and career ready.

claims.text: Twenty-first-century skills depend on high verbal abilities, which are themselves dependent on a shared inventory of knowledge and words.
claims.source_passage: Those skills depend upon high verbal abilities, which in turn depend upon possessing the shared inventory of knowledge and words that make Standard American English useable and effective.

claims.text: The Common Core language arts standards should be interpreted as requiring an elementary curriculum that fosters enabling knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The language arts standards of the Common Core are best interpreted as requiring an elementary curriculum that fosters such enabling knowledge.

claims.text: All modern national cultures are multicultural, artificial constructs sustained by schools and mass communication.
claims.source_passage: All modern national cultures are multicultural. Philosopher and social anthropologist Ernest Gellner brilliantly showed that they are artificial constructs sustained by schools and mass communication.

claims.text: Modern nations utilize a common written language, sustained by dictionaries and schools, to break the traditional connection between language and tribe.
claims.source_passage: To achieve this social-political result, the traditional connection between language and tribe had to be broken. In the eighteenth century new modern nations devised a common written language as the basis for the new national political entity, artificially sustained by dictionaries and schools.

claims.text: The American 'multicultural' campaign of the 1980s and 90s was a conscious political effort to diminish male WASP dominance and elevate minorities.
claims.source_passage: The slow mixing process in America became both conscious and political in the 1980s and 1990s, as part of a leveling campaign to diminish male WASP dominance, and to elevate the status of women and nonwhite, non-Anglo minorities.

claims.text: Terms enter the national 'cultural literacy' list based on their frequency of use by writers and schools without further definition.
claims.source_passage: If schools taught it across the land, and if print and TV writers and now bloggers used the phrase often without defining it, then indeed it would come to appear on such a list.

claims.text: Educational inequality is diminished by teaching 'have-nots' the specific knowledge that the 'haves' currently possess exclusively.
claims.source_passage: My practical suggestion was that a sense of community would be served and inequality diminished if the schools taught everyone, both the haves and have-nots, the knowledge that the haves alone currently possessed.

claims.text: The knowledge differential between social classes is a major cause of the income differential.
claims.source_passage: I claimed, and still do, that this knowledge differential is a major cause of the income differential. (That there is a strong correlation no one denies.)

claims.text: The 1987 Cultural Literacy list described the actual knowledge of literate, high-income Americans rather than a theoretical ideal.
claims.source_passage: The list at the back of the book was actually a list of what literate Americans who made a good living and could communicate with other Americans in fact did know in 1987, but which less fortunate Americans, who did not make a good living, tended not to know.

claims.text: Teaching the specific knowledge possessed by the middle class to all students is a means to diminish inequality.
claims.source_passage: My practical suggestion was that a sense of community would be served and inequality diminished if the schools taught everyone, both the haves and have-nots, the knowledge that the haves alone currently possessed.

claims.text: Possessing a specific shared vocabulary provides economic prowess because it enables communication and learning.
claims.source_passage: People who have that vocabulary are privileged partly because they possess that vocabulary. It enables them to communicate and learn, giving them economic prowess.

claims.text: Educational changes to the shared vocabulary must not come at the expense of depriving poor children of current intellectual currency.
claims.source_passage: To change that vocabulary is a fine thing to do, so long as poor children are not disenfranchised and kept poor in the meantime by being deprived of that powerful income producer.

claims.text: Skills-based multiculturalism has failed to benefit the social groups it was intended to help over the last thirty years.
claims.source_passage: The alternative—skills-based multiculturalism—has, after all, been tried over the past thirty years with little help to the groups it aimed to benefit.

claims.text: When a multicultural term becomes part of shared national knowledge, it is no longer 'multicultural' in the strong sense of being separate.
claims.source_passage: But note that when such language politics is successful, and the phrase catches on, it is no longer exactly 'multicultural'; it has then become part of what we know in common.

claims.text: The culture of the home is distinct from the common language required in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: The culture of the home is not the public sphere. The public sphere deploys a common language.

claims.text: So-called 'WASP language' is multicultural in its origins and not inherently tied to a specific race or gender.
claims.source_passage: So-called WASP language was already multicultural in its origins. It was not inherently WASP or male.

claims.text: Cultural essentialism is a form of tribalism that contradicts the aims of the American experiment.
claims.source_passage: Cultural essentialism is tribalism—the very thing the American experiment was created to eliminate.

claims.text: Multiculturalists of the 80s and 90s incorrectly believed students could function in society as separate cells if they possessed critical-thinking skills.
claims.source_passage: These cells could then all function together if students achieved critical-thinking skills. But neither the critical-thinking idea nor curricular fragmentation has worked out for the social groups that these ideas were supposed to help.

claims.text: Liberal democracies are particularistic in that they require a specific, shared communicative instrument.
claims.source_passage: Liberal democracies are particularistic in their own special way. They each include a particular shared subset of elements that are often wildly diverse in their origins. Each national democracy has its own particular language as a communicative and unifying instrument.

claims.text: Equality of opportunity depends upon all citizens having complete access to shared national knowledge.
claims.source_passage: All citizens should have complete access to that shared knowledge. Equality of opportunity depends upon it.

claims.text: The multiculturalist movement was animated by a protest against particularism, seeking equal status for groups previously discriminated against.
claims.source_passage: It was a protest against particularism—that is, against some narrow, tribal group taking charge, instead of equal status being open to all, including those who had been discriminated against.

claims.text: Multicultural societies are themselves particularistic, and liberal democracies include a specific shared subset of diverse elements.
claims.source_passage: But as it was put into effect, a subtle truth was lost—as such truths tend to be in fierce activisms and polarizations. That truth is that multicultural societies are particularistic too. Liberal democracies are particularistic in their own special way. They each include a particular shared subset of elements that are often wildly diverse in their origins.

claims.text: Each national democracy possesses its own particular language that serves as a communicative and unifying instrument.
claims.source_passage: Each national democracy has its own particular language as a communicative and unifying instrument that is changing, but at any period it is what it is.

claims.text: The public sphere is sustained by the vocabulary and conventions of Standard American English and the communal knowledge that enables its use.
claims.source_passage: But there is also the public sphere, sustained by the vocabulary and conventions of Standard American English, and the communal knowledge that enables its effective use.

claims.text: Proficiency in the language of the public sphere bestows financial and communicative advantages to all who master it.
claims.source_passage: Proficiency with that instrument bestows financial and communicative advantages to all who master it.

claims.text: Schools deny students equality of opportunity if they do not provide the knowledge needed to wield the language of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: If schools do not provide that knowledge, they are denying their students equality of opportunity.

claims.text: Romantic multiculturalism must be revised to prevent the economic exclusion of those who have not mastered shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Though well meant, romantic multiculturalism needs to be revised and transformed so it doesn’t perpetuate the economic exclusion of those who have not mastered the shared knowledge required by effective reading, writing, speaking, and listening.

claims.text: A shared school curriculum is the primary historical instrument of national unity and equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: A well-rounded, constantly updated school curriculum that transmits the shared knowledge of the public sphere has always been the primary instrument of national unity and equal opportunity.

claims.text: The Common Core's skill-based provisions can work against its goals of providing a rich, coherent curriculum.
claims.source_passage: I have shown that its skill provisions are less than ideal, and they sometimes work against its provisions favoring a rich, coherent curriculum.

claims.text: Common Core's current testing models are as unproductive as the failed tests under No Child Left Behind.
claims.source_passage: Its current tests are no more productive in principle than the kinds of tests that have already failed under No Child Left Behind.

claims.text: The greatest virtues of Common Core ELA are increasing text difficulty and requiring that half of the texts be informational.
claims.source_passage: The greatest virtues of the Common Core ELA Standards are their raising of the vocabulary difficulty of texts at each grade level, and their insistence that half of the texts be “informational.”

claims.text: Common Core can only become transformative if districts and states implement a specific, grade-by-grade knowledge-building curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Districts and states can make the Common Core ELA Standards transformative, should they choose to do so, by proposing a specific and coherent curriculum that builds knowledge systematically from one grade to the next, starting in the earliest grades.

claims.text: Current state and Common Core language standards are vague 'Rorschach blots' that require specific content to become reality.
claims.source_passage: Our state language standards and Common Core language standards are currently Rorschach blots that must be formed into a reality, whatever name we give them.

claims.text: Higher achievement and reduced inequality are only possible through specific content standards.
claims.source_passage: It’s the only feasible route to higher average achievement and a reduction of educational inequality. Specificity and courage are needed.

claims.text: The practice of marking texts with highlighters in early grades is an ineffective strategy for increasing knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: The task of close reading in early grades has caused a big boost in the sale of highlight markers, which enable children to practice marking up their books. That is not a very good way to increase knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: Test makers are morally blameless for providing texts on unknown domains because the school curriculum is unstated.
claims.source_passage: Since that uncertainty is not their fault, the test makers are also morally blameless in this situation. They are compelled to offer texts on domains of knowledge not studied in school, since the school domains are unstated and unknown.

claims.text: State-of-the-art translation programs like Google Translate fail at understanding because the relationship between close reading of words and actual meaning is inherently indeterminate.
claims.source_passage: That’s why state-of-the-art translation programs such as Google Translate are so unreliable. They are very good at close reading, but poor at understanding, since the relation between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.

claims.text: The Bible contains no positive uses of the word 'imagine' or its derivatives.
claims.source_passage: No positive uses of the word imagine are to be found in the Bible. Imagination is most likely to be an evil lie, given the unsavory character of unredeemed human nature.

claims.text: Sir Philip Sidney's theory of literature posits that its essence is fixing moral truth in the mind more effectively than history or philosophy.
claims.source_passage: The sixteenth-century English poet and scholar Sir Philip Sidney said that the very essence of good literature is that it fixes moral truth in the mind and heart better than history and philosophy, not that it invents some new world.

claims.text: The elementary school acts as a 'single channel' of shared experience that all children must listen to for several hours every day.
claims.source_passage: The elementary school is a single channel that children must listen to many hours of the day. It is still the place where all children have the possibility of learning the shared knowledge and values that make a nation operational.

claims.text: Standard Spanish is globally updated and fixed by a specific two-volume dictionary from the Academy in Madrid.
claims.source_passage: Standard Spanish, all over the world, at least in its formal written manifestations, is still fixed and updated by the two-volume dictionary published by the Academy in Madrid, Diccionario de la lengua española de la Real Academia Española.

claims.text: The United States was historically conceived as an 'internal empire' that should tolerate local attachments while spreading systematic ideas.
claims.source_passage: There have been in history benign empires (the Roman Empire at times) that did not force people to give up their local languages, modes, and customs, and the United States has been one of them. The United States was conceived as a mutually beneficial internal empire of quasi-sovereign states.

claims.text: Membership in the public sphere's language community is automatically granted to anyone who possesses the necessary shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Membership in the language community of the public sphere is granted to any and all who possess the knowledge needed to wield that instrument of power and opportunity.

claims.text: Schools must prioritize knowledge-centric statements in the Common Core over skill-centric ones if they expect to see superior results in twelfth-grade students.
claims.source_passage: Those knowledge-centric statements are the ones that must predominate if progress is to be made. If the Core standards that focus on knowledge are interpreted as the dominant ones, overshadowing the statements about skills, then we can expect twelfth-grade results to be superior...

evidence.description: Excerpts from the Common Core State Standards documents regarding the need for content-rich curriculum and systematic knowledge building.
SAT scores data showing that while more diverse students are taking the test, results indicate a lack of progress in verbal ability.
evidence.source_reference: Slate article (2015) based on College Board data

evidence.description: W. K. Wimsatt's observation that the importance of close reading depends on the genre and that the linguistic surface of a novel may be less important than its substance.
Research on situational models and mental simulations in discourse comprehension.
Increased sales of highlight markers as a proxy for the shift toward mechanical close-reading practices in schools.
The political reality that specified text lists (besides the Declaration of Independence and Gettysburg Address) would prevent state adoption of standards.
The definition of reading as silent listening, making it topic-dependent.
evidence.source_reference: Hyein Cho et al., 'Internally Generated Conscious Contents,' 2014

evidence.description: Declining or stagnant scores of seventeen-year-olds on the NAEP and SAT despite years of skills-based instruction.
Research on reciprocal teaching showing that the small boost from 'main-idea' practice is a one-time orientation rather than a scalable skill.
evidence.source_reference: Rosenshine and Meister, 'Reciprocal Teaching: A Review of the Research' (1994)

evidence.description: Observational evidence from the Internet and classrooms showing 'close reading' resulting in over-highlighted and over-annotated texts.
An experiment by Rolf Zwaan where close-readers remembered words better but understood meanings worse than a group reading naturally.
evidence.source_reference: Rolf A. Zwaan, 'Effect of Genre Expectations on Text Comprehension' (1994)
evidence.source_passage: The first group could remember the words very well, but they could not remember the meanings as well as the group who read for meaning but did not close-read.

evidence.description: Google Translate as an example of a system that is excellent at surface text processing (close reading) but fails at understanding meaning.
evidence.source_passage: They are very good at close reading, but poor at understanding, since the relation between close reading and meaning is inherently indeterminate.

evidence.description: PARCC Grade 5 practice test items asking for word meaning and the 'clue' or textual feature supporting it.
evidence.source_passage: Part A. What does the word misery mean as it is used in the sentence? ... Part B. Which detail from the story provides the best clue for the mean- ing of the word

evidence.description: PARCC Practice Test Question for Grade 5 involving the interpretation of the word 'misery'.
Research correlating schoolbook simplification with the decline in SAT-Verbal scores.
evidence.source_reference: Hayes, Wolfer, and Wolfe, 'Schoolbook Simplification and Its Relation to the Decline in SAT-Verbal Scores' (1996)

evidence.description: Teacher reports indicating that students can handle more challenging texts than previously believed.
Study demonstrating that syntactic and lexical complexity effects on comprehension are mitigated by familiarity with the topic.
A study demonstrating that student ability to handle syntactic and lexical complexity improves as they become familiar with a specific domain of science.
evidence.source_reference: Diana J. Arya, Elfrieda H. Hiebert, and P. David Pearson, 'The Effects of Syntactic and Lexical Complexity on the Comprehension of Elementary Science Texts' (2011)

evidence.description: Research showing that narratives have a 'privileged' status in human cognition and are more easily understood and remembered than other formats.
evidence.source_reference: Daniel T. Willingham, 'The Privileged Status of Story,' American Educator (2004)

evidence.description: Analysis of the opening passage of 'To Kill a Mockingbird' showing it contains dense references to the Battle of Hastings, Methodism, John Wesley, and regional history.
evidence.source_reference: Harper Lee, To Kill a Mockingbird (1960)

evidence.description: The writings of Sir Philip Sidney asserting that literature aims at fixing moral truth.
The content of Noah Webster’s Blue Back Speller and W. H. McGuffey’s Eclectic Readers.
The 1850s debate over whether to include fairy tales in American schools.
Lexical analysis of the word 'imagine' in the King James Bible.
Susan Ohanian's critique of the 'Standardistos' who rob children of dreams.
Longitudinal Surveys of Youth data linking communication skills to wealth and civic activity.
2011 Achieve Report showing high public approval of the Common Core principle despite low awareness of specific details.
The 1851 record 'The Common School System of the State of New York' documenting early American communal education goals.
The widespread use of 'Everyday Classics Series' textbooks in the 1930s as a de facto national curriculum.
The establishment of Spanish and French national academies to standardize language.
The work of Dr. Johnson and Noah Webster in creating dictionaries to standardize the English language in England and America.
The ongoing role of the Royal Academy in Madrid in fixing and updating the standard Spanish dictionary.
evidence.source_reference: Diccionario de la lengua española de la Real Academia Española

evidence.description: The sociolinguistic and psycholinguistic insight that language normalization requires the normalization of unspoken knowledge.
Antonio Gramsci's critique of the 'new education' and the 'spontaneity' of child development as a 'church' paralyzing research.
Antonio Gramsci's observation from his 1929 prison notebook regarding education as a struggle against biological nature.
evidence.source_passage: In reality, each generation educates and forms each new generation. Education must oppose the elemental biological instincts of nature; it is a struggle against nature, to dominate it and produce the ‘up-to-date’ person of the new era.

evidence.description: George Washington’s last will and testament advocating for a national educational plan to spread 'systematic ideas' and eliminate local prejudice.
evidence.source_passage: It has been my ardent wish to see a plan devised on a liberal scale, which would have a tendency to spread systematic ideas through all parts of this rising Empire, thereby to do away local attachments and State prejudices

evidence.description: Ernest Gellner's social anthropology research on the artificial nature of modern nations.
evidence.source_passage: Philosopher and social anthropologist Ernest Gellner brilliantly showed that they are artificial constructs sustained by schools and mass communication.

evidence.description: The etymological history of American English, including words like 'gumbo' and Native American place names as evidence of an artificial, multicultural common language.
evidence.source_passage: In America, this common language contained all sorts of multicultural elements, with words and idioms from multiple languages, not just from Rome and Greece, but also from Native Americans (especially in place names) and from the Caribbean and African languages (e.g., gumbo).

evidence.description: The adoption of 'Cinco de Mayo' into common American culture and school celebrations as an example of language politics and cultural absorption.
The presence of words like 'chutzpah' (Yiddish), 'gumbo' (Bantu/French), and 'jazz' in standard American vocabulary to prove multicultural origins.
Stagnation of the achievement gap and failure of social groups despite thirty years of skills-based multiculturalism.
The historical failure of testing regimens under No Child Left Behind to produce significant learning gains.
Common Core's specific requirement that 50% of texts be 'informational' rather than purely literary.
Nathan Glazer's analysis of multiculturalism in 'We Are All Multiculturalists Now'.
evidence.source_reference: Nathan Glazer, We Are All Multicuturalists Now (1998)

evidence.description: Shakespeare's Julius Caesar is used as a rhetorical closing to emphasize human agency over deterministic standards.

concepts.author_definition: Segments of the Common Core Standards that explicitly recognize the need for a content-rich, intentionally structured curriculum to build knowledge.
Educational standards that specify skills to be mastered but do not name the specific factual content or topics to be taught at each grade level.
A technique emphasizing conscious attention to the surface features and medium of a text, which Hirsch argues is often misused in K-12 education.
concepts.common_alternative: A general skill for deep understanding of any text regardless of background knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: A measure of text difficulty typically operationalized through quantitative scores like Lexiles, which the author argues is insufficient without content coherence.
The unspoken set of knowledge and vocabulary shared by all competent readers and writers in the public sphere, distinct from a literary canon.
concepts.source_passage: On the other hand, there is a canon of knowledge and vocabulary known to all highly competent readers and writers. That is axiomatic; possessing that knowledge is what makes them competent.

concepts.author_definition: Tests that are composed of passages taken from specific knowledge domains taught in a local core curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: Standardized tests based on 'unfamiliar' or 'cold' passages intended to measure general skills.

concepts.author_definition: An inferential oral ability developed in infancy through speech that is mistakenly treated as a discrete, trainable reading skill for school-aged children.
concepts.common_alternative: A transferable cognitive strategy used to identify the primary focus of any text.

concepts.author_definition: The mental construct of the situation described by a text, upon which contextual (rather than logical) inferences are based.
concepts.common_alternative: Not commonly used in standard 'skills' education; replaces the idea of 'logical inference' from words.
concepts.source_passage: The inferences we usually draw from a text are not logical but con- textual—dependent upon the situation model that we construct, not upon the mere words on the page.

concepts.author_definition: An inappropriate description of the interpretive process in reading, which Hirsch argues is actually contextual and part-whole, not formal logic.
concepts.common_alternative: A standard cognitive skill where readers deduce unstated information directly from textual clues.
concepts.source_passage: the inferences one draws in interpretation are not logical, except in a few specialized texts involving syllogisms or other formal principles. Most inferences are part-whole types that do not depend upon logic.

concepts.author_definition: A technical measure of text difficulty (based on factors like sentence length and word frequency) that is often used as a substitute for defining specific content knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A tool used to ensure students are reading material appropriate for their technical skill level.

concepts.author_definition: The practice of tailoring reading materials to the specific level or interest of individual students, which the author argues prevents the formation of a shared classroom knowledge base.
The practice of using a sequence of texts focused on a single topic or subject area to build familiarity and enable the mastery of complex language.
The theory that stories and narrative structures are naturally suited to the human brain's processing mechanisms, making them the most effective way to transmit information.
Hirsch argues this is not a general ability but is actually a function of domain familiarity; knowing the subject matter is the 'skill' that allows one to handle difficult texts.
Historically, all excellent and memorable writing across genres; later restricted by Romanticism to imaginative fiction and poetry.
An educational goal originating in Romanticism that prioritizes the child's internal imaginative development over communal knowledge.
A pejorative term used by Common Core critics to describe those who support standardized informational text requirements.
The Romantic educational philosophy that focuses on the unfolding of the child's unique nature and imagination.
The deliberate, invented standardized English language developed by Samuel Johnson and Noah Webster to create a shared national culture.
The assumption that imagination is always positive and the primary goal of development, contrasting with the communal/factual goal of education.
The period in American education (specifically mid-1930s) prior to the full implementation of child-centered individualism.
English that is substantially uniform in spelling, grammar, pronunciation, and vocabulary across a nation.
The national dimension of communication involving books, newspapers, public meetings, commerce, and legislatures where one interacts with strangers.
concepts.source_passage: that is the language of the American public sphere: of books, newspapers, public meetings, commerce, and legislatures.

concepts.author_definition: A 'curious aberration' in educational theory that views the child's brain as a naturally unwinding ball of string rather than something needing active socialization and formation.
concepts.common_alternative: Natural child development or student-led learning.
concepts.source_passage: It produced curious aberrations like ‘spontaneity,’ which supposes that the child’s brain is like a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.

concepts.author_definition: A reproach used to discourage the teaching of a communal, shared curriculum, which Hirsch argues is misplaced when applied to the public sphere of a democratic 'internal empire.'
concepts.common_alternative: The imposition of one culture's values and knowledge over others.
concepts.source_passage: We must not be intimidated by the reproach that imparting the communal knowledge of the public sphere is “cultural imperialism.”

concepts.author_definition: A nation conceived as a union of quasi-sovereign states intended to protect security and prosperity while leaving private affairs alone, requiring shared 'systematic ideas' to function.
The deliberate attempt to change or expand the shared knowledge and vocabulary of a nation through schools and media.
A romantic or 'tribal' view of language and culture that treats social groups as separate, unchanging 'cells' that cannot or should not be integrated into a common public sphere.
concepts.common_alternative: Often used by multiculturalists as a way to preserve distinct cultural identities.

concepts.author_definition: The characteristic of having a specific, unique set of shared elements; Hirsch argues that democracies are 'particularistic' because they rely on a specific shared language.
A form of multicultural activism that, while well-intentioned, risks economic exclusion by neglecting the necessity of shared national knowledge.
The author's metaphor for current educational standards that are so vague they can be interpreted in any way by the viewer, lacking objective content.
A metaphor for the elementary school as a shared, universal communicative environment that all children attend simultaneously.
A political entity that protects security and prosperity while allowing private affairs and local customs to remain autonomous.
The specific shared knowledge and vocabulary that functions as a requirement for entry into the middle-class income level.
The shared language of the public sphere used to communicate with strangers and large audiences, typically taught in schools.

cases.name: Common Core State Standards Adoption
cases.description: The process by which over forty states adopted a set of standards that omitted specific content to avoid political conflict over a national curriculum or literary canon.
key_passages: They knew that including definite text titles would exclude gaining the adherence of over forty states, a remarkable achievement.

cases.name: Implementation of Common Core in Early Grades
cases.description: Hirsch observes teachers advising each other on techniques like highlighting every word or heavily annotating margins in young children's books to satisfy 'close reading' standards.
key_passages: I find teachers advising each other about how to get their students to pay close atten- tion to the words of the text. I find images of young students’ books with every word highlighted; a printed page that was originally white is now mainly red or yellow.
contestable_aspects: Whether these Internet-sourced teacher tips represent standard or recommended practice under Common Core.

cases.name: PARCC (Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers)
cases.description: One of the two primary Common Core test consortia. The author uses their Grade 5 practice test as evidence of the 'mirage' of logical inference in close-reading questions, where students are expected to deduce word meanings from context clues that are actually insufficient without prior knowledge.
key_passages: The Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers (PARCC), one of the two Common Core test consortia, has created two-part close-reading questions.

cases.name: Plants and Chlorophyll Unit
cases.description: A hypothetical example of a two-week curriculum unit used to demonstrate how students who start unable to read high-Lexile texts on a topic become capable of reading them after sustained exposure to that specific domain.
key_passages: Under a coherent common curriculum, texts with high Lexile scores might have been unreadable for many students at the beginning of a two-week unit on a domain like “Plants and Chlorophyll,” but will become highly readable by the end of the unit.

cases.name: US Schoolbook Shift (1850s)
cases.description: The transition in American literacy materials from the knowledge-rich, moral, and patriotic focus of Webster and McGuffey to a focus on 'cultivating imagination,' marked by the victory of fairy tale proponents.
key_passages: In earlier times, the readings in Noah Webster’s Blue Back Speller and W. H. McGuffey’s Eclectic Readers were directed to making moral and patriotic citizens.
The fairy tale proponents won out in due course, because by the 1850s “cultivation of the imagination” had started to become an accepted educational goal.

cases.name: 1930s Tennessee Schooling
cases.description: Hirsch recalls his education where local history (localism) was balanced by communal textbooks like the Everyday Classics Series (federalism), prior to the 1930s curriculum reforms toward individualism.
key_passages: Localism in Memphis was acknowledged through the teaching of Tennessee history (which I scarcely remember). Federalism was acknowledged through the use of deliberately communal textbooks

cases.name: European National Academies
cases.description: Spain and France used national academies to institute standardized national languages to unify the nation-state.
key_passages: In Spain and France the job was done in national academies—still very much in existence.

cases.name: The 'Multicultural' Campaign of the 1980s/90s
cases.description: A deliberate effort in the US to expand the canon and shared vocabulary to include women and non-Anglo minorities, which resulted in additions to the 'cultural literacy' list like the term 'Anglo.'
key_passages: The slow mixing process in America became both conscious and political in the 1980s and 1990s, as part of a leveling campaign to diminish male WASP dominance, and to elevate the status of women and nonwhite, non-Anglo minorities.

cases.name: The 'Cultural Literacy' List (1987)
cases.description: Hirsch reflects on the list in the back of his original book, noting it was frequently 'pirated' and misinterpreted as an elitist inventory rather than a descriptive map of the knowledge required for middle-class economic participation.
key_passages: The list at the back of the book was actually a list of what literate Americans who made a good living and could communicate with other Americans in fact did know in 1987
the book itself was not much read compared to the list at the back, which circulated widely in pirated packets

cases.name: Common Core ELA Standards
cases.description: The set of educational standards adopted by most US states. Hirsch evaluates them as having potential (through informational text and vocabulary requirements) but failing in implementation due to a lack of specific content and a reliance on 'skill' rhetoric and flawed testing.
key_passages: I have shown that its skill provisions are less than ideal
The greatest virtues of the Common Core ELA Standards are their raising of the vocabulary difficulty of texts at each grade level, and their insistence that half of the texts be “informational.”
Districts and states can make the Common Core ELA Standards transformative... by proposing a specific and coherent curriculum.

thinkers.name: W. K. Wimsatt
thinkers.key_idea: The linguistic surface of a text is not always its most important feature; close reading's utility depends on the genre.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Wimsatt, a famous proponent of New Criticism, to argue that 'close reading' should not be applied universally as a general skill, especially when the substance of a text is more important than its verbal surface.
key_passages: I remember a telling remark on this topic by a guru of close reading, Professor W. K. Wimsatt of Yale. He stressed the importance of genre in whether to apply close reading.

thinkers.name: Daniel T. Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: Reading comprehension is not a general skill and is tied to knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Willingham's work on 'Raising Kids Who Read' to support the domain-specific nature of reading.
key_passages: Daniel T. Willingham, Raising Kids Who Read: What Parents and Teachers Can Do

thinkers.name: Rolf Zwaan
thinkers.key_idea: Meaning and surface word memory are often in competition; close reading can interfere with the construction of meaning.
thinkers.source_work: Effect of Genre Expectations on Text Comprehension (1994)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Zwaan's cognitive science research to provide a scientific refutation of the 'close reading' pedagogical standard.
key_passages: Close reading had produced inferior comprehension and memory for meaning. This, by the way, is one of the few pieces of research on the subject to have come out in a peer-reviewed scientific journal.

thinkers.name: Oscar Wilde
thinkers.key_idea: Readers find in texts what they have already brought to them (specifically regarding Wordsworth).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Wilde's quote as a metaphor for how 'close reading' is used to confirm pre-existing understanding rather than generate new meaning.
key_passages: He found in stones the sermons he had already hidden there.

thinkers.name: P. David Pearson
thinkers.key_idea: Syntactic and lexical complexity are managed by the reader through their familiarity with the subject matter (domain).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Pearson's work to support the claim that domain familiarity is the chief complexity-managing skill.
key_passages: As topics become familiar, student ability to master rarer words and longer sentences (the chief elements of complexity) increases.

thinkers.name: Sir Philip Sidney
thinkers.key_idea: The essence of literature is fixing moral truth in the heart better than history or philosophy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Sidney to argue that literature has traditionally been tied to truth and knowledge, not just fantasy.
key_passages: The sixteenth-century English poet and scholar Sir Philip Sidney said that the very essence of good literature is that it fixes moral truth in the mind and heart better than history and philosophy

thinkers.name: Richard Poirier
thinkers.key_idea: The concept of literature as 'a world elsewhere.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges this imaginative aspect of literature but argues it still depends on real-world knowledge.
key_passages: But even “a world elsewhere” (as literary critic Richard Poirier called such literature)... depend on knowledge of this world

thinkers.name: Susan Ohanian
thinkers.key_idea: Common Core 'Standardistos' are robbing children of imagination and dreams.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Ohanian as an example of the 'hysterical' Romantic resistance to informational text.
key_passages: the Standardistos who rob children of imagination and dreams will end up.

thinkers.name: Noah Webster
thinkers.key_idea: The creation of a standardized American English dictionary to unify national language and culture.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Webster with creating the American half of 'Johnsonian-Websterese' that allows for national competence.
key_passages: his disciple and rival in America, Noah Webster. Today we learn and speak, read and write Johnsonian-Websterese.

thinkers.name: Dr. Samuel Johnson
thinkers.key_idea: The standardization of the English language through individual dictionary-making.
thinkers.engagement: Identified as the foundational figure for modern standardized English.
key_passages: In England it was done by individuals, notably Dr. Johnson

thinkers.name: Ernest Gellner
thinkers.key_idea: The invented and deliberate character of shared national cultures and nation-states.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gellner to support the idea that national identity is a technical, school-created construction.
key_passages: well described in Ernest Gellner’s excellent Nations and Nationalism

thinkers.name: Antonio Gramsci
thinkers.key_idea: Education is a struggle against biological nature to produce an 'up-to-date' person, and the theory of child 'spontaneity' is an aberration that paralyzes pedagogy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Gramsci to provide a historical and philosophical critique of child-centered 'naturalism,' framing education as a necessary social formation rather than an internal 'unfolding.'
